root.comment:
The key is to realize that if white was on the second row on the right with black to play, white would have a forced win.
Black must block on the lower right, then the king escapes in the upper right corner.

children.comment: Black must block the bottom, otherwise white will threaten either to escape on the lower left, or reach the right part of the board.
Black must block on the right.
White must prevent black from closing in b10
If black blocks on the left, a forced sequence of moves on the right leads to king escape in the upper right corner.

If black blocks the top, playing on the 3rd row, the king threatens the lower left corner, then threatens to go to the right, and then escapes because the 2nd row is open. 
Therefore, black must play on the 2nd row.
White threatens to go to the second row on the right. Black must block otherwise we go back to the variation where black had not block on the right in the first move.
So black must block on the right here. But this allows the king to escape on the lower left corner.

If black plays on the 2nd row, there are still two open routes to the right so white wins by threatening them both.
Same idea whatever black plays on the 2nd left row.
